segments.text: Ia only saw you six or eight months ago.
It's good to see you again.
Thank you.
And we look forward to our interaction.
So we're in your hands.
Tanakotou, Tanakotou, Tanakotou katoa.
I'm John Williamson Tokoingua.
I'm the Chairperson of the Guardians of New Zealand Superannuation.
And I'm joined today by Joe Townsend, our CEO
and Co-Chief Investment Officer, Brad Dunstan.
Also present are Acting GM Shared Services,
Lara Utauta, Head of Sustainable Investment,
Ambrie O'Connor, and Head of Communications,
Katherine Etheridge.
I'm pleased to report to you that the Guardians
has had another strong year with both our mandates,
the New Zealand Superfund and the Elevate New Zealand
Veg Capital Fund, performing well.
At the age of 22, the New Zealand Superfund
has delivered another year of double-digit returns.
It's valued at approximately $90 billion
and is globally recognised as one of the world's best performing
sovereign wealth funds, as well as a leader
in sustainable investment.
We are not however sitting on our laurels.
We are focused on doing the best possible job
for New Zealanders.
We are constantly improving and taking steps to ensure
we are well set up to manage a much bigger fund
and one that is delivering positive financial, social
and environmental outcomes in the future.
The $400 million Elevate Fund,
which was launched just over five years ago,
has now invested in 11 venture capital funds,
which have in turn invested more than $800 million
in 150 kiwi companies, including success stories
like Trader Fire and Holter.
For both mandates, there remains much to do.
We play the long game.
For the New Zealand Superfund,
we can take a long-term view of investments
and can invest in a counter-cyclical opportunistic way.
With the Elevate Fund, our role is to provide oversight
on good governance and investment practices.
so that taxpayers can be confident as well managed.
For both funds, not every investment will be successful,
nor will every 12-month period produce a strong financial return.
This is the nature of investing,
the path to success is never smooth,
and it is important to focus on overall results
over the long term.
Thank you for the opportunity to make these few comments
and we look forward to your questions.
OK, I'm going to go to Megan Woods first.
Thank you very much and thank you for the presentation.
I just want to dive into quite a detailed one
on page 15, the investing in New Zealand of your slide deck.
You talk about looking to seek investment opportunities
to meet around infrastructure, real estate,
and other investments, and you point to the Taranaki Offshore
Parliament, the CIP, partnership you've got
in Offshore Wind there.
Can you talk the committee through some of the opportunities
that exist that, where that project currently is,
and what the dependencies are in terms of taking that project forward?
Sure. I will pass to Joe,
but I would start simply by saying that the project
is still on the scoping stages and is progressing very well.
Thank you.
So the joint venture with Copenhagen infrastructure partners
is a classic example of how we would like to invest
in infrastructure in New Zealand.
So it is alongside an operating partner being Copenhagen
who also could provide capital to the project.
So in terms of where that project is at,
there's a number of regulatory frameworks
that need to be put in place for it to actually move forward.
So that's the Offshore Wind consenting regime
we need there to cut and run it?
Yes, so that is the first one with the issuing of permits.
And that legislation, I believe,
is going through Parliament at the moment and potentially...
on track for early 2026, there's a number of other mechanisms which, you know, over time would need
to be considered and that's around supply and actually pricing. So, you know, is it actually
possible to plug 1 gigawatt of wind power into the grid in New Zealand to add to the overall supply
and also because of the intermittent nature of wind, some sort of pricing stability mechanism that
would enable security around the economics to allow for financing of the purchase.
So, it's just an off-take strike price?
Yes, or something. There's various mechanisms, I understand, across the world
that have been employed, but effectively it's to enable some sort of price stability.
It's such an awesome example of what you guys can do with other people and we've seen a large
amount of potential capital investment in this area leave New Zealand over recent months,
so it's great to hear that the COP partnership is still on track.
Yeah, well it's, we've actually been doing the work with COP for a number of years now.
We are aware that a number of other potential developers have left the market,
but we do continue to do that work with CIP.
Thank you.
Debra Russell.
Thank you.
In some ways it would be fun to dig into your investment returns, but you do a marvellous job.
I think every time you've appeared here when I've been here in this committee,
we've seen your investment returns excellent, they outpace, you know, you pop the pack.
So, I just want to say thank you for that work that you do there.
I don't want to just sort of gloss over it so quickly and not dig into it.
I don't imply that it's not important because I'm not going to dig into that.
beti pakelau transfi. I just want to say thank you. Every time I've spoken, you do a marvellous job for us.
So let's get that on the record. I think most people would agree with that, given how you
air-wildfire and all those sorts of things. So having said all that, one of the things I find
really useful about these sessions is just talking about the global risks and the global
environment that you're operating in at the moment. There's too good an opportunity for
us to pass up to hear from you as to what you see the global risks as being.
So I'm looking at, I think, pages about 48, I think, in your report, talking about global trade,
softening consumer demand. I wonder if you could elaborate on those and how they're affecting
your, how you see the world going, a little bit of a future gazing, and how that's going to
affect your investment strategy. Firstly, thank you for acknowledging our performance in the
last 12 months. The thing we do often stress just to be very clear is we'll have good years
and not so good years. And the important thing for people to look at is the long-term trend
of our performance. Which is excellent. Which is also excellent, yeah. Brad, would you like to
give an overview of how we see the world at the moment? Tell us what's going to happen.
If I knew I was going to have to sit here, but what I would say
is if we look forward it's probably important to think about where we've come from. And we've had a
20-year bull market in global equities characterised by very low volatility, a lot of equities
supplied by central banks, and the evolution of technology. If we think about where we are
going forward, it would be, I guess, difficult to think that we would experience another 20 years
of global equity bull markets. And so we think we're going to enter a more normalised period,
probably higher volatility, more
geo-political stress and, I guess, perhaps, higher interest rates and inflation.
I think the one thing about the New Zealand Superfund, as John has mentioned, is we are an intergenerational fund.
We take a very long-term view of the world and, with my experience looking at other investors globally,
that is really a true endowment that we have.
So, the idea for us is to look through a lot of the day-to-day noise that you might read in the media
and invest where we position ourselves and position the portfolio for long-term trends.
So, great. What are the particular sources of instability that you see at the moment?
I think, obviously, geopolitics is one and the other major thing that we think a lot about
is global equity returns with the construct of AI.
And what does that do to productivity globally? What does that do to GDP?
We look at government's balance sheets and how do they repair balance sheets.
But AI is the one thing that is very difficult to place
and how that then interacts with, I guess, global demographics.
Yeah, the AI puzzle is, I can't sort out what's going to happen and I don't have your expertise,
but I don't think the world has made its mind up about AI yet.
No, and it's something we discuss and debate and contemplate a lot at the fund.
The other area I'm particularly interested in is climate change and your climate change investment strategy.
So, obviously, there's a couple of things here.
You're achieving your carbon reduction targets.
So, I guess two things I'd like to talk about.
One is the climate change reporting. So, obviously, your mandate to report on.
um, uh, that you've got to do the, the climate reporting yourselves, um, uh, how that stacks up
with what other funds, uh, where the other funds around the world are also doing that climate
reporting, whether it's what the particular rubs in that area or what are the particular
things that are easy to do in that space. We just, uh, at least, yeah.
Brad, would you like Amarita to step up?
Yeah, we'll ask her, head of sustainable investment, who is-
Yeah, just come to the microphone.
How does the mic, there's-
Thank you.
Yeah.
Thanks, Amari.
Thank you.
Hi, yes, so we've had our climate change investment strategy for around 10 years, nearly 10 years.
Reducing our, um, exposure to risk has been part of the target setting right from the beginning.
Now it is really quite, um, common practice amongst institutional investors and investors
worldwide to set such targets. They do it in slightly different ways, but there is a common threat.
And there has been standards developed over time around the disclosure,
starting off from the, um, TCFD, the Task Force for Climate Related Financial Disclosures.
That's been really important to us as we have looked to assess our own carbon footprint
and therefore carbon risk across the portfolio. We tend to maintain, um, the same weighting in different sectors.
So we are trying to tilt towards those companies that are much better prepared for climate transition.
So this is an important data point for us. So data disclosure is really important across the portfolio
because we've gone from a state of having to estimate quite a lot of that data
to increasingly at being much better quality, at different markets.
the world regulate or as good practice evolves on climate reporting. What I'm seeing more recently
is that reaching into even the private markets. So you're getting very large companies, some customers
of our exporters, even going down to the level of requesting product level carbon data, including
even to factory level. So it's really is sort of beginning to drive through the supply chain
of large multinational companies that are wanting to strengthen their own data and their own reporting.
So it is a trend that is unstoppable, I think. Okay, that's really interesting. There's a whole
lot of stuff sitting in there. One is the just the demand for carbon reporting and carbon
reduction, so that's real. But one of the things I've been seeing, I suppose ever since I've had this,
we've seen a whole lot of countries around the world late on reporting their
NDCs. We're seeing a whole lot of countries around the world not achieving their NDCs. So
in some of those geopolitical stuff and around, we're seeing governments retreating from
hard action on climate change. But you're saying it's still being driven by
companies. Can you expect any insights there? Right, so there's probably several elements. There's
the companies themselves, so they are wanting to manage their policy and transition risk.
And they continue to want to do that because often the large companies operate across many
different markets. So they're managing across different policy and regulatory regimes. However,
we also as in
estas are not just interested in the emissions. We're also interested in different scenarios.
So if policy and steps back, then maybe the physical risks will increase. So we're also
doing scenario testing around the physical risks folks built in already, the climate risks,
and the sort of insurance risks related to that. And so that's the part of the reporting
that we're seeing called scenario analysis. So you're analysing about transition policy,
technology, and physical risks. The final thing I sort of emphasise on that is that
technology is probably advancing at a much greater rate than what we'd anticipated. I mean,
I can't, you're seeing the electrification of transportation, for example. You're seeing
technology costs and renewables coming right down. You're seeing storage
and batteries sort of coming to the fore. So maybe, you know, technology is stepping in somewhat
here. Right, okay. Thank you. I'm going to go to a sub to Dan, but then back to Megan Woods
for subs. Thanks, Mr Chair. And thanks for coming in today. I'm just going back to the AI piece,
because half of your allocation is in equities. And if you take the view from the economists,
so there's a, there's definitely a bubble in the space. And it's a question of
when, not if there's a correction. What can you tell us about that 54% in the exposure to
the tech sector or to high sectors or businesses that have a, that are over-investing in this space?
i miadau i wanataion, agar i-tкиwaja, betu kaitia bika, te kai nilat i maerau i manuo.
Tadeb yw maerau zain, islw i maerau te kata e1 apatama, te te Matthew.
Pa meroi sy'n tekti, maerau zain, te kata yw maerau te merau i manuo.
Pa merau i maerau te merau i maerau merau i manuo.
Paru rata, te kata kaendara.
a'r maithu atu adau maula ditaill.
We do not think that it is our competitive advantage to pick when particular sectors or
stocks are going to peak or start up.
So that's the starting position.
And indeed if we had that view around AI a number of years ago, and we'd explicitly
taken those stocks out, it wouldn't have benefited the fund in the way that
those stocks actually have. Our benchmark is our starting position and so even though there is a
high weighting in those US tech stocks in our benchmark, every time we make an investment
we actually sell down the benchmark. So by virtue of the fact that we have investments in
Datacom for example and Kangaroa Timbalands and you know other investments around the world,
we are actually selling down our exposure to those stocks. The other comment I would just make is that
initially in Phase 1, if we call Phase 1 of AI, it was all about those big stocks that were
developing the large language model. So ChatGPT for example. What we're seeing now is almost like
Phase 2 of AI where companies are actually really experimenting with productivity gains.
wether or not it can be employed to add even further value within organisations.
Even within some of the private markets parts of our portfolios now,
there's a lot of opportunities that are very tech, AI-focused.
We're mindful that it's not just as simple as looking at that passive benchmark in those big US stocks.
Brad would you add anything else from the AI perspective?
I think it's a very fair question.
It's a question when we talk to our peers, everybody is discussing about the US concentration.
As Joe said, we are slightly less concentrated because we have other investments within our private market sector.
We are measured against a global benchmark, so this is really a benchmark construction question
that people like MSI are dealing with currently.
I guess the only other thing I would add is I have never seen a bubble burst
when the world is talking about a bubble.
When no one is expecting.
Thank you.
My question was around AI as well.
There's obviously the global question of Navida and what that's going to happen.
Just thinking about the elevate portfolio that you also have responsibility for
what we're seeing within local companies using the tools that AI
provide in terms of driving innovation and productivity within the kinds of companies that
we want to invest in.
I'm interested to hear if you're saying that as a trend coming through.
I'm sure we are. I'll pass to Brad.
yunaswa'y, te aolabu isi visa. A i i lupi, a bisi p merchant ni a kai marutu at i p workeda Death

57 investments in companies since its start, around $287 million, I think, so it's certainly
operating within that space. I think the other thing to bear in mind is the elevate mandate
is slightly different and the fact that it has a market development as opposed to a
maximised returns.
What's the, I mean, the failure rate within elevate? I mean, you'd expect within any VC
fund that you do, if you, some would say if you're not having failures, you're not
being kind of proactive and innovative enough within that fund. What's the
failure rate across the fund?
I couldn't give you a specific number for elevate in particular, but in general
in VC, the failure rate is going to be somewhere around 25%, so it's very
high out of every 10 investments. You're going to expect probably 20% to do well,
the bust, the bust bulk of them to do very little, and then probably 25%, 30%.
Do you not track that across the, like Puzza, I mean, obviously elevate
invests in VC's, but do you not track that across the elevate fund?
If we can get specific numbers to you across our entire VC.
And as you say, that that's just normal in this world, but it would be interesting to look at.
Thank you.
Thank you, Megan. I've got Dr David Wilson from New Zealand First.
Yeah, just following on from Dr Woods, the elevate fund.

so what we tend to be seeing
with VC managers, and in New Zealand and internationally
is that it depends a bit on the sector
the management team too
and their decisionmaking
Generally not necessarily captured
by climate reporting legislation at the moment
ONG that it is important to their customer base
or the way they want to manage—they might be in real estate—or to their extraordinating opportunity.
Then they will put in the effort to report or start measuring their climate change emissions.

If it's just competition, what is going on at that level and its causes them to turn that way?
just on your review
of the reference portfolio composition just talk to us a little bit more about that and
and what what you're expecting and will there be any significant changes or is this just to help
reinforce that you're in the that you're in the tracking in the positive way.
So we as a board of management team review our reference portfolio every five years
and in this case it's six years because there was a change in investment leadership
in the board personnel last year so Joe and I delayed it for for one year so it's the opportunity
and it's a year-long process it's very later and we will we will conclude that reference
portfolio process between April and June of next calendar year but it's an opportunity
for board of management to review the risk appetite of the fund and and the and the component
parts of that reference portfolio without predicting an outcome my expectation is that it will look
largely similar to the current reference portfolio there will probably be a greater
focus in the next few months on the the foreign exchange hedging aspect of that
reference portfolio okay thank you i'm going to go to Ryan Hamilton oh thank you chair um
two questions if i could around the investing in New Zealand but and i know when we caught up
with you a few months ago you talked about this as well that you've increased the amount of
capital deployed at a greater rate than the growth of the New Zealand economy
to that growth it's been a little bit soft lately but as it picks up will you maintain that
rate over and above you know in terms of investing in New Zealand equities and
how do you like to talk about our New Zealand investments yeah thanks john um so we are very
much an opportunistic investor so we don't have a target amount that we're looking to put
in New Zealand um but if a good investment opportunity comes across our door
well add to the risk-adjusted returns in the portfolio.
We will certainly, you know, we're always open to increasing our investments in New Zealand.
Because the other thing which you may be aware of is this committee will be looking at the infrastructure funding and financing fund,
which has been enabled in New Zealand for sort of five, six years, but had that very low pick-up.
So the Milldown Housing Project, and I think the Wellington Sludge.
But we're looking to increase that enablement and thought from off-ballot sheet, long-term, you know, ACC,
pension fund type investments in New Zealand infrastructure.
That's something that you'll be keeping an eye on as, you know, I guess good stewards of New Zealand Super and opportunities.
We keep an eye on everything.
The thing that, given the size of the New Zealand Super fund, a key focus for us is scale.
We have a need for scale.
Guardians can never sleep.
OK, we're going to go to Todd Stevenson.
Thank you.
And firstly, congratulations.
You had an excellent audit report and just to echo what Dr Deborah Russell said.
You're doing a great job.
So that's great.
I just want to drill in on one small thing.
Back in 2024, you had the consultancy, yeah, WTW,
very good work.
They made kind of like seven recommendations.
I'm just actually smiling.
I'm looking at their kind of summary deck where they say the soft stuff is the hard stuff.
Sounds like an international consultancy, doesn't it?
But it's a couple of their recommendations that I just wanted to get your view on what you've done in response to them.
One was the first one, developing a set of complexity principles and strategies.
And then I'll call it the third one, maturing the insourcing of private market investments.
So just keen to get your comments on those things that they recommended and what you've kind of done in response to those recommendations.
Thank you.
So I will speak about the first one.
1. Komplexity vs Simplification and then I will ask Brad to talk about the co-investing approach that we actually had.
So whilst we haven't actually formally started developing the complexity principles,
we have certainly been talking about the need to simplify at the organisational level for over a year now.
So this is an organisation that has been extremely successful over time and you know it employs a lot of smart people
and so in some areas of the business some processes had become overly complex without necessarily the need to do that.
So we have built into everyone's KPIs in the business and into the team and strategic planning that we're doing
around identifying complexity and actually simplifying or taking out processes where we're actually able to.
Now we're always mindful of managing risk appropriately because in the business that we're in
you know everyone in the business needs to be a manager of risk in the business
but there are some processes that are not for example related to the movement of money that
you know if you have one control to make sure everything's working you don't need another three or four.
So it's as simple as that. The actual objective itself to develop those complexity principles
we're actually having that discussion internally at the moment as part of the development of the next strategic plan
as to whether or not we've now done enough bottom-up work that that will be a relatively straightforward activity for us to actually do.
So you're hoping to put that in the next strategic plan?
Well it's still being developed. We're still talking to the board about it.
I'd just like to ensure you that you know that all of those Willis-Towers, Watson objectives have not been lost.
You know, in some instances we won't necessarily adopt them to the point
that you know Willis Tuhaws Watson might have attended,
but we are being very explicit that we go through the process of you know
settling on our own positions to whether or not those activities are required
which is probably a really good segue into the second part of the question.
The second part of the question
was around the increasing torty of our ensourcing of product market assets,
the granularity of that recommendation was actually a recommendation to consider offshore offices,
and that was largely around being closer to those private markets.
It's certainly the way in which you have seen a lot of the Australian pensions and Canadian pensions
operate, setting offices up in places like New York and London.
For us, we considered that recommendation.
We don't think it's appropriate for our current size to have offshore offices,
and we think it probably also conflicts a little bit with the simplicity and complexity recommendation.
The way in which we look to get global exposure to private markets is through managers,
as opposed to direct transactions offshore.
It's not to say we wouldn't do that.
We have some examples of where we do, but largely we're going to rely on external managers.
OK, that's great. OK, I'm going to go back to Dr Woodson.
Thank you. Just a bit of a new tag that, as well as investing,
the fund also needs to divest, that that's one of the functions that happens.
Just in terms of what is your framework around that,
in terms of how you go about divesting,
do you try and look to divest into New Zealand ownership,
or is the focus on maximising the return for the fund on the investment that's made
and potentially bringing in other benefits, but just...
betu mau detaile around how you approach divestment.
Yeah, so any investor needs to be able to exit an investment,
so I would start by saying it's extremely important
that any investment we go into,
we need to have some sort of exit strategy.
By the way in which we construct our portfolio,
we are consistently comparing any investment
to any particular alternative,
in particular our starting point,
which is the passive portfolio.
So in general what we are doing
is looking at forward expected returns
versus the risk and where any particular investment
doesn't have the sort of risk-adjusted returns
versus this alternative,
that's when we look to sell down
and then recycle that money back into our passive portfolio.
The funds mandate is to maximise return
without undue risk and so we are always looking
to achieve what is the best possible price
for any divestment.
I think the other part of where we would divest
is where something aligns with our ESG values
or we are looking to reposition the portfolio
for some particular thematic.
Yeah, so I guess having that maximising return on investment,
which I think we all want are super fun to have,
is a criteria for investment,
makes some of those partnering with Government
around infrastructure,
investments complex,
when there is a desire to keep New Zealand ownership
in terms of some of those large public infrastructure pieces.
Just ask Joe to answer that.
Sure.
It's all about how the objective of the fund is actually set up.
So at the moment we have an objective
to maximise returns
subject to risk and a number of other matters.
It would be possible for us to operate
with a different objective for certain assets.
Te marimpohat nakengau pakeiaidai varatiai, pakeiaidaiai ganunriaug traction.

is being able to divest and recycle that capital to actually give us the ability to add to future returns.
Thank you.
OK, anyone else?
Just on your 10-year projection or Treasury's 10-year projection that they estimate the fund will grow to be worth more than $150 billion.
What does it sit at at the moment? 80?
Just under 90.
Just under 90.
What's your view on Treasury's estimates that you'll be worth $150 billion in 10 years?
And perhaps what do you see as the risks that might prevent you from getting there?
Or what do you see as the optimism that might be fueling Treasury's forecast?
I'm respectful of Treasury's estimates because it's incredibly hard to estimate.
And I think historically we've run most estimates but that could equally go the other way.
So, as I said at the beginning, we will have good years and bad years.
So we will keep growing and we should keep growing strongly.
The thing that we have flagged is that the headwinds coming at us now, as Brad said,
will be quite a lot tougher than the tailwinds that have been behind us for the last 15-odd years.
I'm not sure I've answered your question.
I think it's incredibly hard for Treasury to estimate demographics.
mabili i analinai i ananda i teikon tani, aand peremaa maia.
But in your view do you see that as achievable,
that estimate, i maenu turi ka tya iai ba iaitai applauding?
But do you see that as foreseeable?
Brad wants to say something.
I'll just say that...
Joe talked before about,
to Todd's question,
about one of the Willis bowel spots and recommendations
around simplification.
That's a significant exercise in the guardians at the moment,
because we do estimate that roughly every 10 years
maithumātiklī dadawan saiais.
We believe there needs to be a better solution to that
than throwing people at the opportunities
just where technology will come in
and simplifying processes.
But we're preparing ourselves for that doubling in size
over a 10 or so year period.
I was just going to say the same thing.
The doubling comes from the fact that we are 80%
of global equities and we have an assumption
that global equities are going to grow
around 6% to 6.8% per annum.
So whether the 150 billion is achievable
completely depends on the assumption
that equity markets will continue to deliver
historic returns.
I think what I said in sort of the opening
remarks is they have delivered significantly
above long-term estimates over the last 20 years
and so therefore we probably should expect
that over the next period
they would deliver slightly under
to maintain their long-term 6.8%.
OK, well you can be assured we're very grateful
that you're also contributing 21.6 billion
in domestic tax over the next 10 years.
So keep up the good work.
Dr Woods?
Just going back to the comment you made
around how it's a significant exercise
to simplify within the guardians.
The work that you're doing there
and not simply throwing people at the problem
in terms of seeking that return.
Can you just talk us through some of
what that work programme looks like?
I just thought, and then I'll pass to Joe.
Yeah, so it's, I'm sorry.
Yeah, it's a significant cultural exercise.
But the challenge that the board gave to Joe
and a relatively new leadership team 18 months ago
was that despite being one of the better performing funds
in the world for the last 10 or 20 years,
we believe that if there was a simplification of process
